locked_facts: Archimedes' principle: buoyant force = weight of fluid displaced: F_b = ρ_f V_disp g
Object floats when ρ_object < ρ_fluid (buoyant force can equal weight)
Object sinks when ρ_object > ρ_fluid (buoyant force < weight even when fully submerged)
For floating object: fraction submerged = ρ_object/ρ_fluid
Buoyant force acts at the centre of buoyancy (centroid of displaced fluid)
Apparent weight in fluid = actual weight - buoyant force
Buoyant force depends on fluid density, not object density
truth_statements: The buoyant force on a submerged or floating body equals the weight of the fluid displaced by the body
An object floats when its average density is less than the fluid density — it displaces just enough fluid so that buoyant force equals its weight
The fraction of a floating object below the surface equals the ratio of the object's density to the fluid's density
Buoyant force is caused by the pressure difference between the bottom and top of the submerged object
Archimedes' principle applies to all fluids including gases — a helium balloon floats in air for the same reason a boat floats on water

aha_moment.what_to_show: Three objects of same size but different densities placed in water: ρ < ρ_water (floats partially submerged), ρ = ρ_water (neutrally buoyant — floats at any depth), ρ > ρ_water (sinks). The fraction submerged is exactly ρ_object/ρ_fluid for the floating one.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Three objects at different levels in water with density ratios displayed — the floating fraction matches the density ratio exactly
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students think heavier objects always sink — seeing that density (not mass) determines floating makes the principle concrete

simulation_states.label: Pressure causes buoyancy
simulation_states.description: A submerged block. Pressure at bottom > pressure at top. Net upward force = buoyant force F_b = ρ_f V g. Pressure arrows shown at top and bottom.
simulation_states.emphasis: Buoyancy comes from the pressure difference — higher pressure at the bottom pushes up

simulation_states.label: Sink, float, or neutral
simulation_states.description: Three objects dropped in water. Steel (ρ > ρ_w): sinks — W > F_b. Wood (ρ < ρ_w): floats — W = F_b at partial submersion. Neutral (ρ = ρ_w): floats at any depth.
simulation_states.emphasis: Density relative to fluid determines whether it sinks or floats

simulation_states.label: Fraction submerged
simulation_states.description: Floating wooden block: fraction below surface = ρ_wood/ρ_water. If ρ_wood = 600 kg/m³, then 60% submerged. Ice in water: 90% submerged (ρ_ice/ρ_water = 0.9).
simulation_states.emphasis: Fraction submerged = ρ_object/ρ_fluid — a precise, measurable prediction

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts object density and fluid density. Object sinks, floats, or is neutral. Fraction submerged calculated. Apparent weight shown on a spring scale.
simulation_states.emphasis: Change densities — see the float/sink transition. Read apparent weight on the scale.

common_misconceptions.correction: It's the DENSITY (mass/volume), not mass alone, that determines floating. A massive ship floats because its average density (including air inside) is less than water. A small steel ball sinks because steel's density exceeds water's.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show a large light object floating and a small dense object sinking

common_misconceptions.belief: Buoyant force depends on the depth of submersion
common_misconceptions.correction: For a fully submerged object, buoyant force depends only on the volume displaced and the fluid density — NOT on depth. F_b = ρ_f V g is constant at any depth (for incompressible fluids). Depth only matters for partially submerged objects.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Push a fully submerged object deeper — F_b stays constant on the force gauge
